replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Pop culture astrology these days is burdened by a number of superstitions. One of the most deeply entrenched of those is fear of Mercury retrogrades. No, Mercury isn't more sensitive to retrograde motion -- all the planets (though of course not the luminaries) are equally affected by retrogradation -- but people have gotten themselves into a swivet over Mercury retrogrades and so make them much worse than they have to be on account of fear.

To use retrogrades constructively, remember that anything having to do with the planet goes well if it can be described using a word that begins with "re-". Thus I always use Mercury retrogrades to re-vise manuscripts, re-view markets, re-submit old projects to new publishers, and so on, and get excellent re-sults. ;-)

comments.title: Dreams Seem Real
comments.content:
Why do dreams not fulfill recreation?

My dreams seem fully real to me. Yet soon after waking I still feel the need for something interesting to have happened recently. Examples: An engaging and informative dinner party will fulfill my conversation need for a week, but an engaging and informative dream does not fulfill my conversation need for an hour after waking. If I ever went skydiving it would fulfill my adventure desire for a year, but flight in a dream only gives satisfaction for a couple of hours after waking. I could attribute this to the quantity of neurotransmitters released during a dream being minuscule, but this seems an insufficient explanation. Why are dream experiences sequestered?
Suggestions on books to enhance dreams would be appreciated.
Commentariat Thoughts Welcomed
-Maroon Laughing Hyena

replies.title: Re: Dreams Seem Real
replies.content: I'll have to leave this one to the commentariat, as dreams aren't one of my fields of study. Anyone?

replies.title: Re: Dreams Seem Real
replies.content:
I don't have much more than speculation to offer on why dream experiences are less satisfying than those in waking life, but I do have a couple of titles that might be of interest:

- Speculation to be taken well-salted: Two main thoughts come to mind on what may keep dream experiences from feeling as satisfying as waking experiences:
1. We've been told our whole lives that "dreams aren't real", and so even though they feel real in the moment and sometimes have profound emotional resonance, we dismiss them, at least subconsciously. I have to imagine that occult training to take imaginal experiences more seriously might help somewhat with that, but it likely takes a while to overcome a lifetime of conditioning.
2. The Jungian school of dream interpretation tends to focus on dreams as the unconscious mind's way of trying to get a handle on things your conscious mind doesn't get yet. To the degree that's true, dreams are "made of" things that are inherently unsatisfactory, so even if the experience within the dream was "wow, what a great conversation", the dream might have been trying to nudge you toward understanding something or taking some action that you still haven't gotten.

- Dreams: Portal to the Source by Edward Whitmont and Sylvia Perera: Written by a couple of Jungian therapists with a fair amount of clinical experience, this one is mostly useful for working out what your dreams might be trying to tell you, rather than for how to have more satisfying dreams, but I have found it very useful for that.

- Creative Dreaming by Patricia Garfield: This one is a touch "new agey", but it has some eminently practical advice on how to keep a dream journal such that you start to remember your dreams more (some tips on how to write in the dark, while still laying down, for one). I never got super far into some of the lucid dreaming techniques laid out in it, but it has helped me to remember a lot more of my dreams, which has helped me notice when I have some that deserve more of the analytical treatment from above. Having lucid dreams might give you more of a feeling of satisfaction, since your conscious mind would have been more involved, but I've never done it consistently enough to be able to say from experience.

Hope this helps, and I'm interested to see what others might say!
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Dreams Seem Real
replies.content:

-Maroon Laughing Hyena

replies.title: Re: Dreams Seem Real
replies.content:
Because in your dreams it is essentially your own brain talking to itself. You're experiencing an evaluation of the waking world which is not the same as being in that world. No matter how vivid the people in your dreams appear they are still figments of the dream rather than the actual person. They are based on your perceptions of that person. That's why real people are far more exciting than what dwells in your imagination (because they won't follow your expectations) and why skydiving in real life is way more stimulating than in a dream because at least in a dream you don't have to worry about what happens if your parachute should happen to fail!

It's also why virtual reality really can't compete with real reality. It's just not the same thing.

JLfromNH/Azure Chattering Orc

replies.title: Maybe it is a simple thing? Re: Dreams Seem Real

comments.title: Affirmations & Ceremony, Maintaining Inborn Sense of Nwyfre, Kid-Friendly Occult Activity
comments.content:
Good evening!

Tonight, one pretty basic question on affirmations and ceremonial magic, and two kid-related.

1) Is it a good, bad, or indifferent idea to use the same intention for a ritual (like, say, the SOP for self-transformation) and as an affirmation used throughout the day in mundane circumstances?

2) In many of your posts and interviews, you've talked about how kids start out perfectly aware of at least some of the subtler planes, but that culture beats it out of them. Other than the intention to "not do that", any thoughts on how I, as a parent, might help my daughter maintain whatever native sensitivity she may have?

3) Also, you may have answered something similar before, but keeping in mind that participating in ceremonial magic is right out until after puberty, what elements of occult training are safe for kids? I would assume that meditation and divination would be safe (though you'd likely have trouble getting a kid to sit still for meditation), and I know you've said the Judson exercise seems to be safe. Basically, I'm trying to anticipate "Daddy, what's that weird stuff you do, and what can I do to join in?"

Not a question, but for anyone interested: one week into my year of "read things by dead folks", I'm doing pretty well - I cheated slightly and finished Star's Reach, which I had started right after Christmas, tore through God is Red, wrapped up Sane Occultism, and am about halfway through both The Guild State and Psychic Self-Defense. Oh, and for fiction, I'm finally reading The King of Elfland's Daughter, so that I don't have to turn in my "fantasy fan" card at the next meeting.

Thanks much for your time and generosity,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Affirmations & Ceremony, Maintaining Inborn Sense of Nwyfre, Kid-Friendly Occult Activity
replies.content:
1) It's an excellent idea. Unity of will is the foundation of power, and the more you focus all your efforts on a single goal, the more certain you are of achieving it.

2) Ask her about her dreams and then listen. Let her know that you pay attention to intuition and hunches, and take hers seriously. If she has a strange experience, take it seriously. If your family has traditional beliefs, teach them to her. If she sees that you take the subtle dimensions of reality seriously, she'll do the same thing.

3) Meditation and divination are great for kids, and the Judson banishing seems to work very well for them. The other thing to introduce early and often is natural magic. Making a protective amulet with herbs, compounding a blessing incense, dressing a candle with oils and burning it (with adult supervision) -- these and other workings like them are great to share with children.

comments.title: Book Queries
comments.content:
Mr. Greer --

It has been a busy week over here, and a very enjoyable one. I have been spending more and more time offline, which is a very pleasant habit to return to. However, my reading has led into three main questions for this week.

1) I asked for your books at the local metaphysical store, but I asked incorrectly and so got back the only "Greer" book they had. While not as good as your own books, obviously, I am wondering about your thoughts, if any, on Tarot for Your Self, by Mary K. Greer.

2) I am enjoying the works of Arthur Machen, and I find his history with the Golden Dawn interesting, if nothing else. Much of his writing reminds me of H.P. Lovecraft, both positively and negatively. Do you have thoughts, if any, on Machen and his work?

3) Finally, I confess some confusion with the study of Gilles de Rais in Las-Bas. While I understand, in some clumsy way, the Catholic philosophy discussed through his actions, the occult significance(s) of his crimes and philosophy remained rather obscure to me. I know, of course, that the Catholic philosophy cannot be undone from the occult significance, but it's picking out the braid that catches me up.

replies.title: Re: Book Queries
replies.content:
1) Mary's books are very well regarded; their approach isn't one that I use, but your mileage may vary.

2) I used Machen as a bit of a punching bag in a couple of my tentacle novels. He reminds you of Lovecraft because Lovecraft was very strongly influenced by him. I enjoy his writing very much but I loathe his philosophy. Have you read this bit from "The White People"?

"What would your feelings be, seriously, if your cat or your dog began to talk to you, and to dispute with you in human accents? You would be overwhelmed with horror. I am sure of it. And if the roses in your garden sang a weird song, you would go mad. And suppose the stones in the road began to swell and grow before your eyes, and if the pebble that you noticed at night had shot out stony blossoms in the morning? Well, these examples may give you some notion of what sin really is."

To Machen, in other words, "sin" consists of anything in the universe getting "uppity," and rising above the station Machen assigns it in the rigid hierarchy of being. Bad dog! How dare you learn to think or talk! His whole oeuvre is pervaded by an overwhelming sense of status panic projected on a cosmic scale, a blind terror that the universe might not behave itself and do what he thinks it's supposed to.

3) Huysmans was a Catholic, not an occultist. His portrayal of magic in La-Bas is very, very Catholic, and rather ignorant.

replies.title: Re: Book Queries
replies.content:
2) Yes, that was what kept me away from him until yesterday. I read "The Great God Pan" several years ago, and found the complete and utter horror around anything even slightly out of place rather nauseating. Machen's feeling of "out of place" is so easily twigged that it almost reads as satire.

Every time I see people online go wild over how socially unacceptable Lovecraft's views were/are, I'm startled they haven't seen Machen, and viewed the equal horror that status panic can get so much worse.

comments.title: Hiker observes ritual sacrifice: fact or fiction?
comments.content:
Hi JMG, thanks for hosting these. I have a question for you and anyone else reading who'd like to weigh in.

I've read many accounts of people encountering creepy things in the woods. Many of these are classic "fish stories." However, there are some commonalities between some accounts that lead me to think that the writers may be telling the truth.

A particular genre within these tales is the "cult encounter." The protagonist(s) are doing something out in the wilderness and they encounter cult members. They may see a group of black-robed people conducting a strange ceremony, sneak up to get a better view, step on a dry twig, alert the celebrants and then flee for dear life. They may observe the robed worshipers departing from the site of a ritual, wisely hide from their view, then go to the place they left to find a clearing filled with the hideous leavings of animal sacrifices beneath trees carved with menacing symbols. One particularly melodramatic account had the hero use his rifle to shoot the high priest of a cult from afar to halt the imminent sacrifice of a young girl.

I strongly doubt the veracity of that last one, but I don't know that these stories are all lies. Has anyone heard about serious, organized practice of black magic in the wilderness? It seems like a logical place to do things you really don't want an audience for. If anyone's heard talk of such doings I'd be interested to hear it, it would help to get confirmation from a perspective other than that of the shocked hiker who stumbles upon a ritual site.

replies.title: Re: Hiker observes ritual sacrifice: fact or fiction?
replies.content: Yes, I've heard of it. If you want to do malefic magic of certain kinds, doing it outdoors is one of the few safe options. Keep in mind, though, that some of the people who claim to see such things are simply seeing Wiccan covens worshipping, and then filling in colorful details in the usual way.

replies.title: Re: Hiker observes ritual sacrifice: fact or fiction?
replies.content: Nathaniel Hawthorne's story "Young Goodman Brown" is a classic account of what is being discussed here.

replies.title: Re: Hiker observes ritual sacrifice: fact or fiction?
replies.content: Not exactly the same scenario, but "might" be related. In 1988 I was 15 years old and had not got into the occult, just yet. That happened the following year when I got a job and started exploring book stores. Anyway, when I was 15 my best friend and I used to do a lot of hiking in the midland region of South Carolina where we lived. One day we found a trail that looked well kept and decided to follow it. The trail ended at a circular clearing. Every tree surrounding the clearing had a stuffed child's toy that was tied to the tree with string that had been put through the head. On top of that (pun intended) hanging from the trees above the clearing were many stuffed children's toys. I remember that many looked like they had been there for years. Both of us got a dark foreboding feeling about the area.We never saw anyone there, and we never went back.

replies.title: Re: Hiker observes ritual sacrifice: fact or fiction?
replies.content:
The dark side occultist stories seem to be picking up popularity again. I do think there are at times malefic magics happen outside in nature, because people can be jerks in the world of magic, just like in the world of "regular" life, people treat each other badly sometimes.

I've seen evidence of magic in urban nature at one of the places I like to visit: a particularly old oak tree in a cemetery near my home. I've seen what I take as offerings of eggplant on several occassions there. I never went when someone else was at the tree and I don't know the intent. (Question: JMG, do you know of any use of eggplant in magic?)

At another cemetery once when I was in highschool me and some friends were doing a rite in the middle of the night. Then when we were leaving we encountered another group and they knew the not-English language we were chanting in, and mentioned it to us. It was a tense meeting and we didn't get there contact info. It was really weird, but whatever they were also doing in that cemetery they kept secret.

This year I've been following a vlog called "True Crime Files" by Manny Grossman on the Son of Sam murders and suspected cult activity surrounding those crimes. It was suggested to me by an aunt who turned me on to the Chaos book by Tom O'Neil which I really liked, and I like Manny's investigation too, even if I don't agree with a lot of the conclusions. One of the avenues of investigation is looking into ritual and cultic activity at Untermeyer Park in Yonkers, NY. It seems like there was quite a bit. It seems like some of it was malefic. Was it connected to the Son of Sam? I personally don't know and don't know if we ever will.

That connection to a cult was originally made by Maury Terry in his book The Ultimate Evil. His knowledge of actual occultism was lacking and seemed to tap in more to the Satanic Panic.

In the case of Untermeyer Park it does seem, from news clippings and other sources, a lot of dead animals were found there. That is troubling. Were those events connected to the SOS events? That's what they are trying to find out, mixing in with that investigation, a loathing of global elites, and ideas about various kid trafficking rings, mixed with theories or confabulations about darkside occultism.

Anyway, Manny has some good people on his team, including a 32 degree mason who knows his esoteric symbolism. Yet that person is also a devout Christian and tends, imo, to interpret things through a very black-and-white vantage point, though he admits not all occultists are satanists -at least he gets that!

Yet their investigation does seem to think anything connected to Thelema = Satanism, because of the fact that some Thelemites do goetic magic, etc.


I think all this relates very much to some things JMG pointed out on this post here:

https://www.ecosophia.net/an-empire-of-dreams/

JMG wrote: "it’s important to keep a close eye on the fringes of contemporary culture, the places where the future is being born out of the surging tides of unreason. One of the things I watch most closely with this in mind is the burgeoning realm of contemporary conspiracy theories. Those reveal far more than the conventionally minded imagine, irrespective of their factual accuracy or lack of same. As Alain de Botton commented of religions, whether conspiracy theories are true or not is far and away the least interesting question about them."

That's one of the reasons I'm continuing to follow the True Crime files of Manny Grossman...keeping an eye on this fringe. As an occultist I don't want people thinking every time I go into nature to do some thing that is actually probably to trees, stones and streams ~ that instead it has something to do with me harvesting adrenochrome from somebody -even if I'm there by myself.

One of the weird things about conspiracies, similar to urban legends, is the way some truth gets mixed in with much exaggeration. As JMG said above sometimes the blanks get filled by a person who isn't really seeing what they are seeing.

Know Wonder

Urban legends are terribly interesting though!

replies.title: Re: Hiker observes ritual sacrifice: fact or fiction?
replies.content:
Malefic urban magic definitely happens. When I lived in the Adams Morgan neighborhood of Washington, DC, it was pretty routine to encounter or hear about curses done in the Santeria tradition, either in Rock Creek Park or somewhere more public in the middle of the night.

I thought they were just one of the weird things that tend to happen in DC.

But then after I'd moved to a city in New England, I almost stepped on top of a group of dead doves, a lighter and some other objects right on the sidewalk a few blocks from my own neighborhood. The doves had definitely be slaughtered and the scene had that distinctive creepy bad-Santeria flavor (or what I associate with that...it may just be Santeria curses are the only kinds of malefic magic I'd encountered knowingly before.) It freaked me out, frankly. That kind of thing seems to be spreading.

The eggplant magic sounds almost charming, though. I know in North Africa vegetables have djinns with personalities, and eggplants are regarded both with affection and suspicion.

-Murky Slithy Tasmanian Devil, aka Ms. Krieger

Where I used to live in Colorado, the caves/rock formations an hour to the north of me had lots of explicitly Satanic graffiti. The caves/rock formations an hour to the south of me didn't have any graffiti, but I once found a decapitated goat head tucked in a little cranny. The graffiti included some complex sigils and lesser known "infernal names," and the goat head had a cleanly cut neck with no other bones or signs of an animal attack or slaughter, and was pretty far from any farms or ranches. I assume something less than wholesome had gone on in one or both places.

On the other hand, our local pokenose neighbor started spreading rumors about a neighbor practicing "witchcraft" and "worshiping the devil." I assumed she had seem one of the huge number of rituals from several different traditions I was practicing and hosting on a more-than-daily basis at that point. It turned out she was talking about the neighbor next to me--their teenage daughter had a habit of going outside when the weather was nice, putting in earbuds, and dancing to music no one else could hear, which was apparently what the pokenose thought witchcraft looked like.

Said pokenose also liked to spray bleach on the barren dirt in her yard to make sure there were no "germs living there," so you know how it goes.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I think you're missing the point here. It's not whether drugs are good or bad in some abstract sense -- I'll gladly leave that to the moralists on both sides. The question is whether drug use plays well with Western ceremonial magic, and the answer is no. It's been tried repeatedly since the late 1700s, using just about every drug you can think of -- the 19th century Hermeticist Anna Kingsford, for example, used to get stoned on chloroform -- and the results are reliably bad: on the one hand, the states of consciousness achieved are no higher or more useful than the ones you can get without fogging up your mind with drugs; on the other, the psychiatric casualty rate is extremely high.

As for shamanism, if you want to go off and apprentice yourself to a traditional shaman, go through all the necessary training and initiatory experiences, and do it that way, I have no objection at all -- in fact, I think it's a wonderful idea. That shaman won't teach you Western ceremonial magic. If occultism and shamanism are accessing the same spaces by different means, the fact remains that they're different means, and trying to do both at once is like trying to swim and ride a bike at the same time.

comments.content: How do prayers and rituals for the dead actually help the dead?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
That word "dead" is very sloppy terminology. All that happens at what most people call the moment of death is that you shed your material body. The rest of you is still intact and, in most cases, fully aware of your condition. If you haven't done religious or occult practices, furthermore, you're probably confused and disoriented -- and heaven help you if you didn't believe in an afterlife, and there you are anyway!

If there are people praying for you and doing rituals for your benefit, on the other hand, that affects the nonphysical environment you're in. The material body insulates you from the inner planes to some extent; once you shed it, that insulation goes away, and you can feel the thoughts and emotions of other people and of nonhuman spirits as well. Having people beaming thoughts of love and blessing at you, and calling on spiritual beings who can help you, is a little like being hauled out of the water in which you're floundering, wrapped in a warm blanket, and handed something hot to drink.

comments.title: The occult and Jesus Christ
comments.content:
Hello,

Last week there was a brief discussion about Jesus's mission as seen via occultism. Although I am not a christian, I find the life of Jesus Christ to be quite fascinating. Can we have, one day, an ecosophia post about the mission of Jesus and occultism?

In the meanwhile, any good resources?

Regards

replies.title: Re: The occult and Jesus Christ
replies.content: I'll consider it, if I ever want to cause even more shrieking meltdowns than usual; it's a heated issue for many people, after all. In terms of good resources, Dion Fortune's Mystical Meditations on the Collects is a good place to start; another very interesting work is Rudolf Steiner's Christianity as Mystical Fact. From a different perspective, Morton Smith's Jesus the Magician is well worth a read.

replies.title: Re: The occult and Jesus Christ
replies.content:
To our host's excellent recommendations I would add Margaret Barker's The Great High Priest: The Temple Roots of Christian Liturgy (2003).

Barker is a very interesting scholar, a Methodist from the non-liturgical wing of Protestantism. Depsite her personal church affiliation, she profoundly appreciates what ancient Christian (especially Eastern Orthodox) liturgies can tell us about the doctrines and practices of Jesus himself. She has a thorough command of all the primary sources in almost all of the original ancient languages. (She is quite elderly now, and has not published very much in the last few years.)

To briefly summarize her extensive body of work, she has concluded--I think, rightly concluded--that Jesus and the earliest Christians came out of an unwritten tradition of pre-Rabbinic Judaism that had preserved and valued the rituals and practices of the First Temple (in sharp opposition to those of the Second Temple, which that tradition regarded as a wholly illegitimate, false Temple).

Moreover, she argues that the ritual tradition of the First Temple had significant points of contact with the Greek ritual practices that the Neo-Platonists later called theurgy. Historically, the influence runs from the First Temple traditions (and the similar traditions of other ancient Temples in the Near East) to Plato, not the other way.

She suspects (as seems reasonable) that this school originated in the First Temple's high-priestly families, who passed down through the generations their tradtions of what they regarded as the true worship of Israel, which Ezra and his ilk had badly corrupted when they and their followers returned to Jerusalem from the Babylonian captivity.

All this is a enormous and radical departure from conventional Western scholarship, whether Jewish or Christian, but I find her general arguments quite convincing.

She also offers some evidence that Plato was influenced by this same high-priestly Temple traditions, especially in his "Timaeus."

She also argues, and I am absolutely convinced by her arguments here, that the canonical books of the New Testament do not present the beating heart of early Christianity. They are, for her, merely exoteric works; the esoteric teaching of Jesus and his disciples and heirs was much richer, and (like Plato's exoteric teaching) was never meant for public consumption. And, apart from faint echoes in early Christian liturgies and in the spirtiual disciplines of a few ancient Eastern monastic communities (Mt. Athos and Mt. Sinai, chiefly), by now that esoteric teaching of Jesus has been wholly lost--lost beyond any hope of recovery. The Protestant ideal of sola scriptura "by Scripture alone" does not and cannot take Christians back to the beating heart of Jesus' teaching.

(This is where she shows herself to have the temperament of a true scholar: she accepts that what she most wishes to know has been lost forever, and that she can never know it. A con-artist among the scholars--and they are very many--would have cobbled something together out of hints and guesses, and claim to have discovered the Truth at last.)

As one might guess from this summary, mainstream (Western) scholars, especially Protestant ones, have found it hard to deal objectively with her published works, since they strike at the very roots of their scholarship, and also (in many cases) of their personal religious faith. Some Eastern Orthodox scholars, however, seem to appreciate her scholarly work.

replies.title: Re: The occult and Jesus Christ
replies.content:
Thank you to robertmathiesen for the lead on Barker. Her work seems fascinating.

It's interesting to see her conjecture about the temple as a model of the macrocosm/microcosm, which is analogous to some efforts in Renaissance magic to model spaces after the cosmos ("Ma tempio farò il cielo, altar le stelle..." Tommaso Campanella)

Axé,
Fra' Lupo

replies.title: Re: Astrocartography
replies.content: I haven't found it very useful. The method I use to check a potential location requires a fair amount of astrological background, but if you have that, it works well. I cast my natal chart as though I had been born at the moment that I was actually born (whatever that works out to in the new time zone), but in the place I want to consider. That keeps the planets in the same signs and degrees but in different houses, which makes a lot of difference. (For example, you can move a malefic into a cadent house where it's weak, or move a benefic into an angle where it's strong; you can also change house rulers and thus reshape your life -- for example, on the east coast I've got a horoscope that makes it easy to make money, while on the west coast it's a horoscope that predicts poverty. Yes, I'm prospering here in Rhode Island -- why do you ask? ;-)

But I worry that visualizing such a future (especially when done consistently - and on the macro scale- by large numbers of people) could magically influence events in a "negative" way-- ie maybe steering us toward a darker future than is perhaps absolutely necessary. The media doesn't help in this regard, but I feel like staying abreast of current events is kind of a survival tactic in a chaotic situation such as this. Catch 22.

Do you ever wrestle with this? What's the best way, on your opinion, to approach this apparent friction- both collectively and in one's daily personal life? Put another way- can you offer advice on cultivating the healthiest mix of fatalism/acceptance vs. active intentional influence of events?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Let's put the same question on a different scale. You and I are both going to get old and die. Is it harmful to reflect on that, come to terms with it, and make plans for dealing with it? Not in any way I can see. Quite the contrary, it's the people who refuse to deal with those realities who generally make the biggest messes of their lives, and those who deal with it in a creative and constructive fashion that lead lives that make a positive impact on the world.

Civilizations, like individuals, have a life cycle. They are born, they grow, they reach maturity, they age, and they die. That's hardwired into the basic structures of human social reality. That being the case, the same rule applies. The longer we pretend that our civilization is uniquely precious and thus destined to go zooming off forever into the stars, the more we can be sure that it's old age and death are going to be far more miserable for ourselves, the rest of our species, and the planet than they have to be. The sooner we come to terms with the inevitable, and start dealing with it creatively, the better the results for everyone will be.

replies.title: The three angelic hierarchies
replies.content:
In Eastern Orthodox/Roman Catholic tradition there are three angelic hierarchies, each consisting of three choirs:

1. First hierarchy, devote themselves with the worship of God himself, dwell mostly in his presence. Loving Seraphim, wise Cherubim, preeminent Thrones.

2. Second hierarchy, devote themselves with keeping the order of the Cosmos, tending to planetary intelligences, fighting off forces of Chaos, etc. Dominions, Virtues and Powers.

3. Third hierarchy, devote themselves with human kind. It is my layman opinion that it is not that they are low because they are connected to us. They were tasked to deal with us because, being the least developed of the hierarchies, it is more natural to them to express their thoughts/intentions in a way that can be parsed by human minds. The choirs are:

a. Principalities: They are spirit guides of whole nations. If you consider the US to be a Christian country, you might conceive Lady Liberty to be one of those.

b. Archangels: They are "chiefs of angels". I have never read a discussion of what they actually *do*, but if you go by Scripture, named angels (such as Michael and Gabriel) do interact with individual humans that have a disproportionately high weight on history. You could speculate that they may deal with groups of people smaller/other than nations.

c. Angels: They work with and guide individual humans. These are your Holy Guardian Angels.

replies.title: Re: Reincarnation
replies.content:
I'm not sure what JMG will say, but I'll put it this way: either the dream that fits a bunch of the usual past life dream criteria isn't actually a past life dream (and it fits a *bunch* of the usual criteria: a dream occurring very young in the wake of a minor brain injury [I was four at the time]; remembered with crystal clarity decades later despite both how early in my life it occurred and the fact that I rarely remembered my dreams at that age; there's a family story concerning my comments in the wake of that injury because those comments are woefully disproportionate to the severity of the accident but quite consistent with the events of the dream itself; and a couple of my enduring interests make a lot of sense in light of that dream), or my "last" incarnation occurred at least a billion years in the future (and given some of my other impressions I have my doubts), or something really weird went on sometime in Earth's past, or the answer to your question is yes - if that's an actual past life dream, then wherever my last incarnation was, it was somewhere where the rising stellar primary was much redder than the Sun today and covered somewhere between a tenth and a quarter of the horizon when rising. (I clearly remember thinking of the star as a red giant, and my impression is that it was more specifically in the late stages of becoming a red giant - which was steadily rendering the planet I was on uninhabitable due to water bodies starting to evaporate, and which is why I doubt this was Earth because the current research suggests that Earth will become uninhabitable due to water evaporation from increased solar output well before the Sun actually starts becoming a red giant. It might be Mars even further into the future; otherwise I was in another solar system entirely.)

While my experience is probably uncommon even if it's realy (or possibly was uncommon until the human population explosion really started in earnest), I suspect I'm not the first to do this - while there are differences, the passageway I remember taking to get here is similar enough to the sipapu of Hopi myth that I would not be surprised if at the souls of least some of the ancestors of that tribe got here the same way, and quite possibly some of the other Native tribes of the southwestern US and Mesoamerica as well. (I suspect that kind of crossing requires specific astrological conditions on the outlet side to occur, and I also doubt it's a coincidence that Mom had me when she was older and that I was born by C-section after difficulties giving birth.)

(The implications of the old occult lore about demons being beings from a previous universe in the light of this is something I am still mulling over.)

(Side note #1: I tried to probe that dream in meditation once. I got what I can only describe as a pillar of salt warning. So apparently that is a thing that can happen under the right circumstances.)

(Side note #2:

There is an old motif, in the stories I told myself when I was young, of a pilgrimage through seven worlds. A child's story... except when I got older I realized that in those seven worlds I had reinvented large parts of the astrological symbolism of several planets, and elements of those stories have shown up in places they have no business doing so. And there's a resemblance between this and some of the old Theosophical lore. So perhaps not.

One of those worlds, I suspect, corresponds to Earth. It is, not the fifth, but rather the sixth world in the sequence. (More interestingly to me, the fifth corresponds to Mars... and my memory of wherever my last incarnation was did give me a Martial impression, even if it's not Mars itself.)

And I note that the Lemurian and Atlantean cycles both ended in cataclysm, and that industrial civilization sure seems to be headed the same way.

I would not be at all surprised if part of the reason I am here is that the fifth civilizational cycle has either already ended or is about to do so, leaving us at the start of the sixth.)

- pretentious_username

comments.title: Autogenic training

replies.title: Re: Autogenic training
replies.content: I don't have any experience with it, and would also be interested to hear if it has occult connections.

replies.title: Re: Autogenic training
replies.content:
I've put far more time and effort into experimenting with both it and hypnosis than was probably wise. I actually started using it when recovering from some really severe injuries but dropped it in favor of other methods.

Autogenic training seems to work fairly well on its own, but it also seems to play badly with most systems of magic. The basic technique of reciting a verbal formula in order to induce a change of state in yourself is good, and can be used independently of full autogenic training.

I don't think there is an occult connection; if there was it was mist likely lost. Vogt, whose research served as the basis of the whole thing was a militant materialist. He actually disliked hypnosis and autosuggestion for being too focused on the mind. Schulz was trying to give patients the ability to fix themselves. His dream was that his six volume text on the subject would become part of the school curriculum and the world would be free of disease. Despite a heavy dose of physical culture, there didn't appear to be anything overtly occult leaning in the text. Personally, I think Luthe was an idiot. Vogt and Schulz were keenly aware of paroxysmic discharges; avoiding it was one of the key aims of there methods. Given that he was also a big fan of Vogt, and ties himself in knots trying to come up with a material rationale for the phenomena.

TLDR; mixes badly with magic, no occult connection, would not recommend

comments.title: forbidden archaeology
comments.content:
Dear JMG,
You provide such a great forum here, and I am coming to you as a last hope of getting a straight answer on this:

Awhile back, in answering a young commentor interested in the archaeology and ethnology of the Americas, you mentioned the institutionalized resistance to the idea that the ancestors of native Americans got here by any other way than over the land bridge between Siberia and Alaska. When I was a young art student fascinated by the similarities between the arts and cultures of ancient China and those of meso-America, I was given a momentary dose of the public ridicule and gas-lighting that has kept any professional from pursing the subject—and the result is that I have spent a lifetime trying to figure out what is going on with this.

SO—do you, or does any of your readership, know WHY it is such a thought-crime to talk about Asian and European contributions to New World Pre-Columbian culture and genetic make-up?

replies.title: Re: forbidden archaeology
replies.content:
It's not a matter of there being some overriding reason. Once the dominant figures in the archeological community signed onto it, it became the orthodoxy, and any attempt to suggest anything else was squelched by them and their many flunkeys as a threat to their control over the discipline. It's exactly the same way that kept Noam Chomsky's universal grammar -- which is complete garbage, by the way; it just barely seems to work if the only language you know is English -- stuck in place as dogma in the field of linguistics: Chomsky and his flunkeys were better at academic infighting than their rivals, got control of the major journals, and treated any alternative idea as a threat to be stomped.

So you noticed the astounding parallels between Shang-dynasty Chinese art and Olmec art! Glad to hear it. It's one of many examples; you might also compare Shang art to the Northwest Coast native traditions; there are other remarkable parallels.

replies.title: Re: forbidden archaeology
replies.content:
Chomsky was quite gifted at the dirty art of securing personal dominance within an academic discipline--in his case, linguistics. His position as a major grant-getter at MIT helped him enormously in this. He was able to exericise some control over the flow of grant money to other scholars in his field, and also over who would get hired for one or another of the few academic positions open in that field. He had ways of making it very clear to his minions who was in and who was out of his favor at any given time. At one point his students would refer to his annual course critiquing new developments in linguistic theory as his "enemies course," and if they were near MIT, they would sit in on it from time to time just to keep up with who was currently in favor with the Big Man, and who was not. I was teaching Slavic linguistics at Brown University in those years, just an hour's drive down the interstate from MIT; and I was a tenured non-Chomskyan linguist, so I heard a good deal of gossip while remaining safe from his ire.

As to the broader question of academic orthodoxies: In Germanic legends, the powerful warrior (or maybe he was even a king) Gunther fell afoul of Attila the Hun, who had him tossed into a pit of deadly vipers. (Gunther called out for his harp, and played his death-song on it with his feet--his hands were bound--as the vipers bit him to death.) After a lifetime in academia, I can only compare it to Gunther's snake-pit. There are many worthy scientists and scholars in the country's colleges and universities, but the institutions themselves are pretty tightly focussed on their revenue-streams and their public images. And thus an appearance of absolute certainty is the coin of the academic realm.

Some of it is much less rational than what I just wrote. Every troop of Chimpanzees--our closest biological relatives--has a dominance hierarchy with an alpha male at its apex, and beta males allowed to jocky for position among themselves so long as they don't annoy the alpha. Scientists in any academic discipline quite often behave just like a troop of chimpanzees.

One of the most curious things of all about Chomsky's long career is that very many of the early grants he received came from the Department of Defense and the associated military and intelligence sectors of the Federal Government, even though the man himself was a very outspoken Far Leftist in his political writing. Gives one pause, it does!

And our host is absolutely right about the worth(lessness) of Chomsky's Universal Grammar, IMHO.

comments.title: Household protection
comments.content: When we do a SOP or a Sphere of Light etc we are clearing space, banishing unhealthy influences etc around us.  Is there an equivalent for clearing / protecting the area in and around your whole house/garden?

replies.title: Re: Household protection
replies.content: You can make the Sphere as large as you want it to be. Doing it around an entire house is good exercise.
